title: Clerk authentication issue with Next.js middleware
body: I'm having trouble configuring Clerk with Next.js middleware. I want to protect all routes except `/` and `/sign-in`. My middleware isn't redirecting unauthenticated users properly and I keep getting redirected to the sign-in page even when logged in.

replies.body: Make sure your `publicRoutes` array in `middleware.ts` is correctly configured. Clerk's middleware will protect everything not explicitly listed there.
Are you using `clerkMiddleware` or `authMiddleware`? `clerkMiddleware` is the newer approach. Also, check your Clerk environment variables are set correctly.
Sometimes a caching issue can cause this. Try clearing your browser cache or testing in incognito mode. Also, ensure your `NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_SIGN_IN_URL` is set.
Double-check the order of your middleware. If you have other middleware, it might be interfering. Clerk's middleware should typically run early.
Could it be a version mismatch? Ensure your `@clerk/nextjs` package is up to date and compatible with your Next.js version. Breaking changes sometimes occur.
I had a similar problem. My `publicRoutes` array was incorrect. I needed to specify `/` and `/sign-in(.*)` to catch all sub-routes of sign-in too.
Check your `CLERK_SECRET_KEY` is present in your `.env.local` file. Without it, Clerk cannot properly authenticate server-side.
Are you calling `startAuth()` or `protect()` within your middleware? `clerkMiddleware` handles this automatically, but if you're customising, that could be a point of failure.

title: Verifying Stripe webhooks in a Node.js Express app
body: I'm setting up a Stripe webhook endpoint in my Node.js Express app to handle subscription events. How do I properly verify the webhook signature to ensure security? I'm worried about malicious requests.

replies.body: You need to use `stripe.webhooks.constructEvent`. It takes the raw request body, the `stripe-signature` header, and your webhook secret.

Crucially, ensure your Express app parses the raw request body BEFORE any other body parsers. `express.json()` will consume it, so use a specific middleware just for webhooks: `app.use('/webhook', express.raw({type: 'application/json'}))`.
Don't forget to store your `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET` securely in environment variables. Never hardcode it.
Also, consider using a `try...catch` block around `constructEvent` because it will throw an error if the signature is invalid or the event is stale.
What helped me was testing locally with the Stripe CLI. It allows you to forward events to your `localhost` endpoint and handles the signature generation.
Make sure the `webhookSecret` you're passing to `constructEvent` matches the one shown in your Stripe dashboard for that specific endpoint.
If you're using a cloud function or serverless environment, sometimes the raw body parsing needs a specific configuration for that platform.

title: Extending Clerk's user model with custom fields
body: I need to add custom fields like 'companyName' and 'role' to Clerk user profiles. What's the best way to do this with Clerk's API and Next.js? Should I use metadata or create a separate database table?

replies.body: Clerk provides `publicMetadata`, `privateMetadata`, and `unsafeMetadata` for this. `publicMetadata` is best for fields like 'companyName' that clients might need to access.
For 'role', if it's sensitive and only your backend should know, `privateMetadata` is suitable. You can update these via Clerk's Backend API.
You can also use webhooks! When a new user signs up (user.created event), trigger a webhook to your backend, then call Clerk's API to update their metadata.
On the frontend, you can access `publicMetadata` from `useUser()` hook or `currentUser` object. For server-side, `auth()` and `currentUser()` provide access.
If you have complex relationships or very large custom data, a separate database table linked by Clerk's user ID might be better. But for simple fields, metadata is cleaner.
Remember that `unsafeMetadata` is for specific client-side updates where you don't mind users potentially manipulating it. Not recommended for 'role' or critical data.
When updating metadata, make sure you're using the correct `Clerk` instance (backend SDK vs client-side `user.update()`). Be mindful of permissions.
I'd suggest starting with metadata. It keeps your user data centralized. Only move to a separate table if you hit specific scale or relational complexity issues.

title: Handling JWT expiry and refresh tokens in a SPA
body: I'm building a Single Page Application (SPA) with a custom backend authentication using JWTs. What's the recommended approach for managing JWT expiry and securely refreshing tokens without constant re-logging? I want a smooth user experience.

replies.body: The standard approach involves using both a short-lived access token and a long-lived refresh token. Access tokens are sent with every API request.
When the access token expires, your frontend should catch the 401 Unauthorized response, then send a request to a dedicated `/refresh-token` endpoint with the refresh token.
The refresh token should ideally be stored in an `HttpOnly` cookie. This makes it inaccessible to client-side JavaScript, mitigating XSS risks.
Upon successful refresh, the backend issues a new access token (and optionally a new refresh token for rotation). The frontend then retries the failed API request.
Implement refresh token rotation: each time a refresh token is used, invalidate it and issue a new one. This limits the damage if a refresh token is compromised.
Consider a blacklist or revoke mechanism for refresh tokens on the backend, especially when a user logs out or changes their password.
What about automatically refreshing before expiry? You can set up an interceptor that checks token expiry time and proactively calls the refresh endpoint a few minutes before it expires.
Be careful with refresh token expiry. If both access and refresh tokens expire, the user must re-authenticate. Balance security with user convenience.

Don't store JWTs in `localStorage`. While convenient, it's vulnerable to XSS. `sessionStorage` or in-memory (if tokens are short-lived and refresh is frequent) are better if you can't use `HttpOnly` cookies.

title: Ensuring reliable webhook event delivery and handling idempotency
body: My service relies heavily on webhooks from various third-party APIs. How can I ensure reliable event delivery (retries, queues) and handle duplicate events gracefully using idempotency keys? I'm worried about missed events or processing the same event multiple times.

replies.body: For reliable delivery, a message queue is almost essential. Ingest all incoming webhooks into a queue (e.g., AWS SQS, RabbitMQ, Kafka) and process them asynchronously.
This decouples your webhook receiver from the processing logic, allowing you to handle spikes and retry failures without blocking the incoming webhook.
For idempotency, most webhook providers include an `id` or `eventId` in the payload or headers. Store this ID in your database and check if you've already processed it before proceeding.
When processing, wrap the operation in a transaction and check for the `eventId`. If it exists, skip. If not, process and then save the `eventId`.
Be aware of 'at-least-once' delivery guarantees common with webhooks and queues. This means you *will* receive duplicates, so idempotency is crucial.
What about retries? Your queue system (e.g., SQS dead-letter queues) can handle this. If processing fails, the message can be put back in the queue or moved to a DLQ for manual inspection.
You can also implement your own retry logic with exponential backoff for transient errors, but let the queue handle long-term delivery attempts.
Logging is key! Log every incoming webhook, its `eventId`, and the outcome of processing. This helps debug missed or duplicate events.
Consider using a distributed lock (e.g., Redis locks) if your processing involves multiple instances and you need to ensure only one instance processes a unique event.
